title: World War II begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: World War II, or the Second World War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies and the Axis powers.

title: Cold War begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Cold War was a period of international geopolitical rivalry between the United States (US) and the Soviet Union (USSR) and their respective allies, the capitalist Western Bloc and communist Eastern Bloc.

title: Industrial Revolution begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Industrial Revolution, sometimes called the First Industrial Revolution in contrast to the subsequent Second Industrial Revolution, was a transitional period of the global economy toward more widespread, efficient and stable manufacturing processes, succeeding the Second Agricultural Revolution.

title: Middle Ages begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: In the history of Europe, the Middle Ages or medieval period lasted approximately from the 5th to late 15th centuries, comparable with the post-classical period of global history.

title: Renaissance begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Renaissance is a European period of history and cultural movement, very roughly defined as covering the 14th through 17th centuries, though sometimes more narrowly defined for instance as only covering the 15th through 16th centuries.

title: Sumer begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Sumer is the earliest known civilization, located in the historical region of southern Mesopotamia, emerging during the Chalcolithic and early Bronze Ages between the 5th and 4th millennium BC.

title: Age of Enlightenment begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Age of Enlightenment, also known as the Age of Reason or simply the Enlightenment, was a period of intellectual and cultural flourishing in Europe and Western civilization, emerging in the late 17th century in Western Europe.

title: Indus Valley Civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Indus Valley Civilisation (IVC), also known as the Indus Civilisation or the Harappan Civilisation, was a Bronze Age civilisation in the northwestern regions of South Asia, lasting from 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE, and in its mature form from 2600 BCE to 1900 BCE.

title: Age of Discovery begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Age of Discovery, also known as the Age of Exploration, was part of the early modern period and overlapped with the Age of Sail.

title: Gilded Age begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: In United States history, the Gilded Age is the period from about the late 1870s to the late 1890s, which occurred between the Reconstruction era and the Progressive Era.

title: Bronze Age begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Bronze Age is an archaeological and anthropological term defining a phase in the development of material culture among ancient societies in Asia, the Near East and Europe.

title: Victorian era begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: In the history of the United Kingdom and the British Empire, the Victorian era was the reign of Queen Victoria, from 20 June 1837 until her death on 22 January 1901, although slightly different definitions are sometimes used.

title: Cretaceous begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Cretaceous is a geological period that lasted from about 143.1 to 66 Ma.

title: Jurassic begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Jurassic is a geologic period and stratigraphic system that spanned from the end of the Triassic Period 201.4 Ma to the beginning of the Cretaceous Period, 143.1 Ma.

title: Neolithic begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Neolithic or New Stone Age is an archaeological period, the final division of the Stone Age in Asia, Europe, and Africa.

title: Vedas begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Vedas, sometimes collectively called the Veda, are a large body of religious texts originating in ancient India.

title: Belle Époque begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Belle Époque or La Belle Époque was a period of French and European history that began after the end of the Franco-Prussian War in 1871 and continued until the outbreak of World War I in 1914.

title: Minoan civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Minoan civilization was a Bronze Age culture which was centered on the island of Crete.

title: Progressive Era begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Progressive Era (1890s–1920s) was a period in the United States characterized by multiple social and political reform efforts.

title: Mycenaean Greece begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Mycenaean Greece was the last phase of the Bronze Age in ancient Greece, spanning the period from approximately 1750 to 1050 BC.

title: Early modern period begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The early modern period (1500–1800) is a historical period, with divisions based primarily on the history of Europe and the broader concept of modernity.

title: Yamnaya culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Yamnaya or Yamna culture, also known as the Pit Grave culture or Ochre Grave culture, is a late Copper Age to early Bronze Age archaeological culture of the region between the Southern Bug, Dniester, and Ural rivers, dating to 3300–2600 BC.

title: Early Middle Ages begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Early Middle Ages, sometimes controversially referred to as the Dark Ages, is typically regarded by historians as lasting from the late 5th to the 10th century.

title: Interwar period begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: In the history of the 20th century, the interwar period lasted from 11 November 1918 to 1 September 1939 – from the end of World War I (WWI) to the beginning of World War II (WWII).

title: High Middle Ages begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The High Middle Ages, or High Medieval Period, was the period of European history between c. 1000 and c. 1300; it was preceded by the Early Middle Ages and followed by the Late Middle Ages, which ended c. 1500 according to historiographical convention.

title: Late Middle Ages begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Late Middle Ages or late medieval period was the period of European history lasting from 1300 to 1500 AD.

title: Migration Period begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Migration Period, also known as the Barbarian Invasions, was a period in European history marked by large-scale migrations that saw the fall of the Western Roman Empire and subsequent settlement of its former territories by various tribes, and the establishment of post-Roman kingdoms there.

title: Bell beaker culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Bell Beaker culture, also known as the Bell Beaker complex or Bell Beaker phenomenon, is an archaeological culture named after the inverted-bell beaker drinking vessel used at the beginning of the European Bronze Age, arising as early as 2800 BC.

title: Late antiquity begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Late antiquity is a time period between classical antiquity and the Early Middle Ages.

title: Classical Greece begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Classical Greece was a period of around 200 years in Ancient Greece, marked by much of the eastern Aegean and northern regions of Greek culture gaining increased autonomy from the Persian Empire; the peak flourishing of democratic Athens; the First and Second Peloponnesian Wars; the Spartan and then Theban hegemonies; and the expansion of Macedonia under Philip II.

title: Mesolithic begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Mesolithic is the Old World archaeological period between the Upper Paleolithic and the Neolithic.

title: Natufian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Natufian culture is an archaeological culture of the late Epipalaeolithic Near East in West Asia from 15–11,500 Before Present.

title: Corded Ware culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Corded Ware culture comprises a broad archaeological horizon of Europe between c.

title: Ubaid period begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Ubaid period is a prehistoric period of Mesopotamia.

title: Clovis culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Clovis culture is an archaeological culture from the Paleoindian period of North America, spanning around 13,050 to 12,750 years Before Present (BP).

title: Mississippian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Mississippian culture was a collection of Native American societies that flourished in what is now the Midwestern, Eastern, and Southeastern United States from approximately 800 to 1600 CE, varying regionally.

title: Prehistoric Egypt begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Prehistoric Egypt and Predynastic Egypt was the period of time starting at the first human occupation of the region and ending at the First Dynasty of Egypt around 3100 BC.

title: La Tène culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The La Tène culture was a European Iron Age culture.

title: Hallstatt culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Hallstatt culture was the predominant Western and Central European archaeological culture of the Late Bronze Age from the 12th to 8th centuries BC and Early Iron Age Europe from the 8th to 6th centuries BC, developing out of the Urnfield culture of the 12th century BC and followed in much of its area by the La Tène culture.

title: Uruk period begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Uruk period is a period of the protohistoric Chalcolithic to Early Bronze Age in Mesopotamia and the ancient Near East.

title: Aurignacian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Aurignacian is an archaeological industry of the Upper Paleolithic associated with Early European modern humans (EEMH) lasting from 43,000 to 26,000 years ago in most areas and lasting until about 17,000 years ago in Ukraine in the form of the Epi-Aurignacian.

title: Acheulean begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Acheulean, from the French acheuléen after the type site of Saint-Acheul, is an archaeological industry of stone tool manufacture characterized by the distinctive oval and pear-shaped "hand axes" associated with Homo erectus and derived species such as Homo heidelbergensis.

title: Caral–Supe civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Caral–Supe was a complex pre-Columbian era society that included as many as thirty major population centers in what is now the Caral region of north-central coastal Peru.

title: Andronovo culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Andronovo culture is a collection of similar local Late Bronze Age cultures that flourished c.

title: Cucuteni–Trypillia culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Cucuteni–Trypillia culture, also known as the Cucuteni culture or Trypillia culture is a Neolithic–Chalcolithic archaeological culture of Southeast Europe.

title: Oldowan begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Oldowan was a widespread stone tool archaeological industry during the early Lower Paleolithic spanning the late Pliocene and the first half of the Early Pleistocene.

title: Magdalenian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Magdalenian is a technocomplex of the Late Upper Paleolithic in parts of Western and Central Europe.

title: Early Dynastic period begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Early Dynastic Period is an archaeological culture in Mesopotamia that is generally dated to c. 2900 – c.

title: Urnfield culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Urnfield culture was a late Bronze Age culture of Central Europe, often divided into several local cultures within a broader Urnfield tradition.

title: Moche culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Moche civilization flourished in northern Peru from about 100 to 800 AD, during the Regional Development Epoch.

title: Nuragic civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Nuragic civilization, also known as the Nuragic culture, formed in the Mediterranean island of Sardinia, Italy in the Bronze Age.

title: Mousterian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Mousterian is an archaeological industry of stone tools, associated primarily with the Neanderthals in Europe, and with the earliest anatomically modern humans in North Africa and West Asia.

title: Gravettian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Gravettian is an archaeological industry of the European Upper Paleolithic that succeeded the Aurignacian circa 33,000 years BP.

title: Vinča culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Vinča culture, also known as Turdaș culture or Vinča-Turdaș culture, is a Neolithic archaeological culture of Southeast Europe, dated to the period 5400–4500 BC.

title: Nok culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Nok culture is a population whose material remains are named after the Ham village of Nok in southern Kaduna State of Nigeria, where their terracotta sculptures were first discovered in 1928.

title: Naqada III begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Naqada III is the last archaeological phase of the Naqada culture of ancient Egyptian prehistory.

title: Space Age begins
subtitle: Historical era
fact: The Space Age is a period encompassing the activities related to the Space Race, space exploration, space technology, and the cultural developments influenced by these events, beginning with the launch of Sputnik 1 on October 4, 1957.

title: Lapita begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Lapita culture is the name given to a Neolithic Austronesian people and their distinct material culture, who settled Island Melanesia via a seaborne migration at around 1600 to 500 BCE.

title: Linear Pottery culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Linear Pottery culture (LBK) is a major archaeological horizon of the European Neolithic period, flourishing c.

title: Nazca culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Nazca culture was the archaeological culture that flourished from c.

title: Unetice culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Únětice culture, Aunjetitz culture, or Unetician culture is an archaeological culture at the start of the Central European Bronze Age, dated roughly to about 2300–1600 BC.

title: Hohokam begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Hohokam was a culture in the North American Southwest in what is now part of south-central Arizona, United States, and Sonora, Mexico.

title: Funnelbeaker culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Funnel(-neck-)beaker culture, in short FBC, TRB or TBK, was an archaeological culture in north-central Europe.

title: Old Europe begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Old Europe or Danubian civilisation was a relatively homogeneous pre-Indo-European Neolithic and Copper Age culture or civilisation in Southeast Europe, centred in the Lower Danube Valley.

title: Erlitou culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Erlitou culture was an early Bronze Age society and archaeological culture.

title: Mogollon culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Mogollon culture is a pre-historic archaeological culture of Native American peoples from Southern New Mexico and Arizona, Northern Sonora and Chihuahua, and Western Texas.

title: Solutrean begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Solutrean industry is a relatively advanced flint tool-making style of the Upper Paleolithic of the Final Gravettian, from around 22,000 to 17,000 BP.

title: Chavín culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Chavín culture was a pre-Columbian civilization, developed in the northern Andean highlands of Peru around 900 BCE, ending around 250 BCE.

title: Paracas culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Paracas culture was an Andean society existing between approximately 800 BCE and 100 BCE, located in what today is the Ica Region of Peru.

title: Painted Grey Ware culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Painted Grey Ware culture (PGW) is an Iron Age Indo-Aryan culture of the western Gangetic plain and the Ghaggar-Hakra valley in the Indian subcontinent, conventionally dated c.1200 to 600–500 BCE, or from 1300 to 500–300 BCE.

title: Chernyakhov culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Chernyakhov culture, Cherniakhiv culture or Sântana de Mureș—Chernyakhov culture was an archaeological culture that flourished between the 2nd and 5th centuries AD in a wide area of Eastern Europe, specifically in what is now Ukraine, Romania, Moldova and parts of Belarus.

title: Kura–Araxes culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Kura–Araxes culture was an archaeological culture that existed from about 4000 BC until about 2000 BC, which has traditionally been regarded as the date of its end; in some locations it may have disappeared as early as 2600 or 2700 BC.

title: Jastorf culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Jastorf culture was an Iron Age material culture in what is now Germany, stretching north into Jutland, and east into Poland, spanning the 6th to 1st centuries BC, forming the southern part of the Pre-Roman Iron Age.

title: Villanovan culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Villanovan culture, regarded as the earliest phase of the Etruscan civilization, was the earliest Iron Age culture of Italy.

title: Jemdet Nasr period begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Jemdet Nasr Period is an archaeological culture in southern Mesopotamia.

title: Iberomaurusian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Iberomaurusian is a backed bladelet lithic industry found near the coasts of Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia.

title: Globular Amphora culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Globular Amphora culture (GAC, German: Kugelamphoren-Kultur ; c.

title: Tumulus culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Tumulus culture was the dominant material culture in Central Europe during the Middle Bronze Age.

title: Serednii Stih culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Sredny Stog culture or Serednii Stih culture is a pre-Kurgan archaeological culture from the mid. 5th – mid. 4th millennia BC.

title: Cycladic civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Cycladic culture was a Bronze Age culture found throughout the islands of the Cyclades in the Aegean Sea.

title: Wari begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Wari were a Middle Horizon civilization that flourished in the south-central Andes and coastal area of modern-day Peru, from about 500 to 1000 AD.

title: Lusatian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Lusatian culture existed in the later Bronze Age and early Iron Age in most of what is now Poland and parts of the Czech Republic, Slovakia, eastern Germany and western Ukraine.

title: Baden culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Baden culture or Baden-Pécel culture is a Chalcolithic archaeological culture dating to c.

title: Dong Son culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Đông Sơn culture, Dongsonian culture, or the Lạc Việt culture was a Bronze Age material centered on the Red River Delta in northern Vietnam.

title: Maykop culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Maykop culture or Maikop culture, c.

title: Halaf culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Halaf culture is a prehistoric period which lasted between about 6100 BC and 5100 BC.

title: Chachapoya culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Chachapoyas, also called the "Warriors of the Clouds," were a culture of the Andes living in the cloud forests of the southern part of the Department of Amazonas of present-day Peru.

title: Samarra culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Samarra culture is a Late Neolithic archaeological culture of northern Mesopotamia, roughly dated to between 5500 and 4800 BCE.

title: Przeworsk culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Przeworsk culture was an Iron Age material culture in the region of what is now Poland, that dates from the 3rd century BC to the 5th century AD.

title: Gerzeh culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Naqada II refers to the second Pre-dynastic archaeological stage centered around the Naqada region of Upper Egypt.

title: Helladic period begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Helladic chronology is a relative dating system used in archaeology and art history.

title: Catacomb culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Catacomb culture was a Bronze Age culture which flourished on the Pontic steppe in 2,500–1,950 BC.

title: Maglemosian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: Maglemosian is the name given to a culture of the early Mesolithic period in Northern Europe.

title: Aterian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Aterian is a Middle Stone Age stone tool industry centered in North Africa, from Mauritania to Egypt, but also possibly found in Oman and the Thar Desert.

title: Saqqaq culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Saqqaq culture was a Paleo-Eskimo culture in southern Greenland.

title: Karasuk culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Karasuk culture describes a group of late Bronze Age societies who ranged from the Aral Sea to the upper Yenisei in the east and south to the Altai Mountains and the Tian Shan in ca.

title: Ertebølle culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Ertebølle culture is a hunter-gatherer and fisher, pottery-making culture dating to the end of the Mesolithic period.

title: Amratian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Naqada I culture, originally called the Amratian culture, was the first Pre-dynastic archaeological culture of prehistoric Egypt, centering on Upper Egypt.

title: Chinchorro culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Chinchorro culture of South America was a preceramic culture that lasted from 9,100 to 3,500 years BP.

title: Fatyanovo–Balanovo culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Fatyanovo–Balanovo culture was a Chalcolithic and early Bronze Age culture within the wider Corded Ware complex which flourished in the forests of Russia from c. 2900 to 2050 BC.

title: Quimbaya civilization begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Quimbaya were a small, ancient indigenous group in present-day Colombia noted for their gold work characterized by technical accuracy and detailed designs.

title: Ahrensburg culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Ahrensburg culture or Ahrensburgian was a late Upper Paleolithic nomadic hunter culture in north-central Europe during the Younger Dryas, the last spell of cold at the end of the Weichsel glaciation resulting in deforestation and the formation of a tundra with bushy arctic white birch and rowan.

title: Azilian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Azilian is a Mesolithic industry of the Franco-Cantabrian region of northern Spain and Southern France.

title: Zarubintsy culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Zarubintsy, Zarubyntsi or Zarubinets culture was a culture that, from the 3rd century BC until the 1st century AD, flourished in the area north of the Black Sea along the upper and middle Dnieper and Pripyat Rivers, stretching west towards the Southern Bug river.

title: Clactonian begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Clactonian is the name given by archaeologists to an industry of European flint tool manufacture that dates to the early part of the Hoxnian Interglacial around 424–415,000 years ago.

title: Lengyel culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Lengyel culture is an archaeological culture of the European Neolithic, centered on the Middle Danube in Central Europe.

title: Swiderian culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Swiderian culture is an Upper Palaeolithic/Mesolithic cultural complex, centred on the area of modern Poland.

title: Merimde culture begins
subtitle: Archaeological culture
fact: The Merimde culture was a Neolithic culture in the West Nile Delta in Lower Egypt, which corresponds in its later phase to the Faiyum A culture and the Badari culture in Predynastic Egypt.
